Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only top‑level variable assignments (including the `source` array with an HTTPS URL from the project’s own GitHub releases and two local files) and function definitions (`prepare()`, `package()`). There are no command substitutions, backtick commands, or any executable code in the global scope that would run when the PKGBUILD is sourced. Neither `pkgver()` nor any other function is present that could execute during the sourcing step. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions are not executed by `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo` because of the `--nobuild` and `--noprepare` flags. The download of the AppImage from a standard upstream URL and the inclusion of SHA256 checksums are normal practices. No encoded or obfuscated data, no unexpected network destinations, and no commands that could exfiltrate data or execute untrusted code are present in the scope that will be evaluated.
</details>
<summary>Safe to fetch sources and source PKGBUILD.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a standard .desktop file that defines the launcher metadata for the Stably Orca application. It specifies the application name, generic name, comment, icon, categories, and MIME types. The `Exec` line runs `stably-orca %U`, which is a standard pattern for launching an application with optional URL arguments. There is no obfuscated code, no network requests, no file operations beyond normal desktop entry usage, and no evidence of malicious behavior. The file is perfectly safe.
</details>
<summary>Standard .desktop file with no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Reviewed stably-orca.desktop. Status: SAFE -- Standard .desktop file with no security issues.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD is a standard AUR package for a prebuilt Electron-based IDE. It downloads a specific AppImage release from the project's own GitHub Releases (pinned by version and checksum), extracts it, and copies the files into the package directory. All source URLs point to the upstream repository, and the checksums are fixed (not SKIP). No obfuscated code, suspicious network requests, or dangerous commands (eval, base64, curl into a pipe) are present. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions only handle AppImage extraction, permission fixes, and installation of desktop integration files—expected behavior for a binary package. There is no evidence of supply-chain attack, data exfiltration, or backdoor insertion.
</details>
<summary>Standard -bin PKGBUILD with pinned checksums, no suspicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard -bin PKGBUILD with pinned checksums, no suspicious behavior.

Reviewing additional files...
Reviewing additional files...
[0/1] Reviewing stably-orca.sh...
LLM auditresponse for stably-orca.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a straightforward wrapper script for an Electron-based application (Stably AI Orca). It sets the `APPDIR` environment variable to `/opt/stably-orca` and then executes the contained `AppRun` binary with a `--disable-features=Vulkan` flag. The comments explain the rationale for both actions: avoiding AppRun's auto-detection issue with flag arguments and disabling Vulkan due to incompatibilities with the Wayland backend and NVIDIA rendering.

No malicious behavior is present: there are no network requests, downloads, code execution from untrusted sources, obfuscation, file exfiltration, or system tampering. The script performs only the minimal operations needed to launch the application with appropriate flags. This is a standard packaging practice for distributing pre‑compiled AppImage trees.
</details>
<summary>Safe wrapper script, no malicious behavior detected.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed stably-orca.sh. Status: SAFE -- Safe wrapper script, no malicious behavior detected.
Reviewed all additional files.
Reviewed additional files.
Audit complete! Result: No issues found

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/stably-orca-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-n2vwzfl3...

Analyzing stably-orca-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-n2vwzfl3) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source array with a direct AppImage download from the project's GitHub releases and two local helper files. The `sha256sums` are explicitly provided and match the sources, so there is no use of SKIP or checksum bypass. The source fetching for `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will only download the listed AppImage and include the local files, with no command substitution, variable execution, or remote code inclusion in the global scope.

The `pkgver()` function is not defined, so the static `pkgver=1.4.80` is used. No top-level command substitutions or immediate execution (e.g., `$(...)`, `eval`, or backticks) occur outside of function definitions. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions, while including AppImage extraction and filesystem operations, are not executed due to the `--noprepare` flag and will be audited separately in later stages.

All network sources are from the declared upstream (`https://github.com/stablyai/orca`), and the AppImage URL is a predictable release path using the known version. No obfuscated code, suspicious downloads, or data exfiltration attempts are present in the PKGBUILD's parse-time execution. The package behavior during this narrow phase (source download and sourcing the PKGBUILD) is safe and consistent with standard AUR packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>
Source fetch and parse safe; no malicious execution
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Source fetch and parse safe; no malicious execution[/]

LLM auditresponse for stably-orca.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a standard desktop entry (.desktop) file used by desktop environments to integrate applications into the system menu and handle MIME types. It declares metadata such as the application name, icon, categories, and the command to execute (`stably-orca %U`). The `Exec` field points to the `stably-orca` binary with proper argument handling (`%U` for URLs), which is consistent with standard packaging practices. The `StartupWMClass` ensures correct window grouping, and `MimeType` registers the application to handle `stably-orca` URL schemes—common behavior for Electron applications.

There are no suspicious commands, code executions, network operations, or file manipulations present. The file does not contain obfuscated content or dangerous scripts. It serves only to integrate the Orca IDE into the desktop environment, which aligns with the expected functionality of a .desktop file. All fields are appropriate for the declared application and pose no supply-chain risk.

No evidence of malicious activity or deviation from standard desktop entry specifications is found.
</details>
<summary>
Standard .desktop file, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed stably-orca.desktop. Status: SAFE -- Standard .desktop file, no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves a prebuilt AppImage from the project's official GitHub release page using a versioned URL (`https://github.com/stablyai/orca/releases/download/v${pkgver}/orca-linux.AppImage`), which is an expected and standard practice for binary packages. The `sha256sums` are provided and match the AppImage, `stably-orca.sh`, and `.desktop` file, ensuring integrity. The file is not extracted during source unpacking (`noextract`), and proper hashing prevents tampering.

In the `prepare()` function, the AppImage is made executable and extracted using its built-in `--appimage-extract` functionality, which is a common and safe approach to unpack AppImages for repackaging. The `package()` function copies the contents of `squashfs-root` into the package without modifying or executing any binaries. It adjusts permissions to ensure the application is usable by non-root users and installs wrapper scripts and desktop integration files as expected. No obfuscated code, network calls, or suspicious commands (like `curl|bash`, `eval`, or remote downloads) are present. The operations are consistent with standard AUR packaging practices for AppImage-based software.

Icons are installed from the extracted AppImage into the system's hicolor theme in standard sizes, and fallback logic uses a known icon path. This is normal desktop integration behavior. The package conflicts and provides correct naming to avoid collisions. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, injected backdoors, or execution of unauthorized code. All actions serve the legitimate purpose of installing a prebuilt application in a distribution-appropriate way.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AppImage repackaging without malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard AppImage repackaging without malicious behavior
Reviewed all required files.

Reviewing additional files...
Reviewing additional files...
[0/1] Reviewing stably-orca.sh...
LLM auditresponse for stably-orca.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `stably-orca.sh` is a wrapper for launching the Stably AI Orca application from a pre-installed AppImage tree located at `/opt/stably-orca`. It sets the `APPDIR` environment variable to ensure correct operation of the bundled AppRun script, which is standard behavior for AppImage-derived applications.

The script disables the Vulkan feature in Chromium/Electron to avoid rendering issues on NVIDIA GPUs, which is a legitimate compatibility workaround. It optionally supports Wayland via a commented-out exec line, indicating user-configurable behavior rather than hidden functionality. No network operations, file exfiltration, code evaluation, or remote execution is performed.

All operations are confined to launching a local binary with predefined flags and are consistent with normal packaging practices for Electron-based desktop applications. There is no obfuscation, suspicious command usage, or deviation from expected behavior. The decision remains SAFE.
</details>
<summary>
Wrapper script with no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed stably-orca.sh. Status: SAFE -- Wrapper script with no malicious behavior
